narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and backgrounds to forecast first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 39, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both at 2, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 56.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's average of around 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the lower tier compared to peers who generally pass the bar. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your LSAT score of 39, which is stronger than the average of 36.7 and pulled the prediction toward a higher chance of passing by showing solid standardized reasoning skills. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, slightly below the average of 3.21, also nudged things toward passing but with less force, as it indicated respectable but not standout college performance relative to bar passers. While your middle family income and full-time status align with most students, they didn't sway the outcome much here. Overall, your situation compares to typical admitted students by having a competitive LSAT edge, but your lower decile rankings highlight a performance gap that outweighed those strengths in the model's eyes. This doesn't define your potential—many students in similar positions improve dramatically with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking courses or seeking academic coaching to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through a post-bac program, and reapply after demonstrating upward trends in grades. Building a stronger narrative around your resilience as a Black male navigating these challenges could also strengthen future applications, showing admissions teams your commitment to success. With focused steps, you can shift the odds in your favor for the next cycle.
